draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to CRM interfaces that feel like talking to a friend rather than enterprise software
facade.label: Warm and conversational
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into casual language, emoji accents, encouraging tone, human-centered copy
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward formal, corporate language, traditional business UI patterns, minimal personality

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to CRM interfaces that feel like talking to a friend rather than enterprise software
evidence.implication: Lean into casual language, emoji accents, encouraging tone, human-centered copy

facade.hypothesis: Freelance CRM users prefer high contrast between navigation and workspace for task focus
facade.label: Dark sidebar, light content
facade.acceptImplies: Explore darker navigation patterns, possibly dark mode variants with bright content areas
facade.rejectImplies: Explore softer contrast, unified tone, or light-dominant layouts instead

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer scannable card layouts over dense tables for viewing active clients and project status
facade.label: Client list as cards
facade.acceptImplies: Design client views with visual cards showing essential info (name, project, rate, status) rather than spreadsheet-style tables
facade.rejectImplies: Explore table-based or list-based layouts; cards may feel like overkill for quick reference needs

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to calm, structured visual hierarchy that feels predictable and lets them focus—rather than chatty or dynamic interfaces that compete for attention
facade.label: Quiet, organized grid
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward minimalist, restful design; likely values clarity over novelty; may prefer muted colors, generous whitespace, and subtle interactions
facade.rejectImplies: User wants more liveliness, personality, or visual interest; finds blank space boring; may prefer color, motion, playful micro-interactions, or warmth in the interface

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer a persistent sidebar for navigation with card-based layouts for clients/projects, balancing quick access to core functions with visual clarity of individual records.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation persistence vs. card-based organization — sidebar anchors the UI while cards create scannable project blocks
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward persistent navigation + modular card display; probe denser sidebar variants, card grid options, and what goes in each card next
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers top tabs, lists, or full-width layouts; pivot toward horizontal nav and linear scrolling patterns

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer fluid, momentum-based scrolling through client lists over instant, rigid paging—reducing cognitive friction when scanning many contacts.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — continuous fluid motion vs. discrete jumps
facade.label: Smooth list scrolling
facade.acceptImplies: Design list interactions with inertial scrolling, spring animations, and gesture-driven momentum. Apply to client browse, project history, invoice timeline.
facade.rejectImplies: Use instant pagination, snappy clicks, or step-by-step navigation. Favor precision over flow. Design for deliberate, non-gestural interaction patterns.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers want CRM interfaces that signal credibility and serious business capability, even if they work solo
facade.label: Strict professional
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into formal language, minimal emoji, structured UI, corporate-adjacent tone to build trust with clients
facade.rejectImplies: The friendly-first approach has stronger product-market fit; overly formal voice may feel cold and distance freelancers from their own tool

axes.label: Whether they prefer minimal, clean interfaces vs. rich visual detail and color—reveals if they want distraction-free focus or visual richness to feel professional
Whether they favor dashboard-centric overview vs. list/table-based workflows—reveals how they mentally organize client and project information
Whether they want a business-formal tone vs. casual, encouraging tone—reveals if they see the tool as a serious business system or a supportive partner
Whether they prefer calm, minimal atmosphere vs. energetic, motivational atmosphere—reveals if they want to feel grounded or inspired while working
Whether they want all client/project info visible at once vs. progressive disclosure that reveals details on demand—reveals tolerance for information density
Whether they prefer instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, animated transitions—reveals if they value speed and directness or polish and delight

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer minimal, clean interfaces vs. rich visual detail and color—reveals if they want distraction-free focus or visual richness to feel professional

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo
